caveats: a: O*NET combines vending with coin and amusement-machine service roles and supplies task presence, not wage-weighted time shares.
a: The occupation profile omits some warehouse, sales, and central-office roles.
a: The frozen compensation ratio is from ancestor NAICS 4451 grocery and convenience retail and may not reflect route driving, technical service, and warehouse labor in vending.
rho: This is implementation judgment rather than an observed five-year realization rate.
rho: High VMS and cashless adoption means some readily captured benefits are already in the baseline.
rho: New AI functionality may be limited by legacy machine connectivity and data ownership.
e: No source directly measures eligibility under the normalized-EBITDA and transferability lens.
e: Many survey respondents combine vending with micro markets, office coffee, pantry, or bottling.
e: The frozen LMM firm-count input is missing, so the eligible-firm count cannot be calculated.
s5: The operator survey is not a probability sample and combines vending with adjacent convenience services.
s5: The owner-age evidence is all-industry, owner-level, and from data year 2018.
s5: The evidence does not provide a unique eligible-firm denominator or isolate external control changes.
q: No source directly measures AI-benefit pass-through in vending contracts.
q: Retention varies with location exclusivity, route density, client subsidy, and whether end users or hosts bear price changes.
q: The estimate excludes demand changes and implementation friction.
d5: The sources mix vending with adjacent formats and report nominal revenue.
d5: The NAMA forecast comes from an industry body and participating companies represent about 27 percent of industry activity.
d5: Office attendance, product inflation, client subsidies, and conversion to micro markets can separate revenue from current-basket quantity.
o: This is a bounded judgment rather than an observed operator-retention share.
o: Technology can change the format from traditional vending to smart cooler or micro market without eliminating the convenience-services operator.
o: The estimate concerns accountable operation, not preservation of route headcount or visit frequency.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Operators earn merchandise gross margin from repeated end-user purchases and commonly secure continuing access to workplaces, factories, schools, healthcare, hospitality, residential, or public locations through service agreements or location commissions; route density and replenishment quality drive economics.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive in-house vending operations; machine manufacturers or one-time equipment sellers; amusement, gambling, personal-service, and DVD machines classified elsewhere; passive machine financiers; shells; and firms outside the normalized EBITDA band.
lens.included_activities: Lower-middle-market U.S. vending-machine operators that repeatedly place, stock, merchandise, collect from, monitor, and service machines at external customer locations under route or location relationships.

a.bridge: O*NET describes coin, vending, and amusement machine servicers across multiple industries rather than the complete wage-weighted workforce of six-digit vending operators. I map recordkeeping, settlement, parts ordering, diagnostic information retrieval, route planning, pre-kitting, demand forecasting, and exception triage to AI-exposed work, while treating filling, driving, loading, cleaning, installation, and most repair as physical and unexposed. This likely understates exposure where operators have substantial dispatch, sales, warehouse, and administrative staff, but overstates it where owner-drivers perform nearly all work physically.
caveats: O*NET combines vending with coin and amusement-machine service roles and supplies task presence, not wage-weighted time shares.
The occupation profile omits some warehouse, sales, and central-office roles.
The frozen compensation ratio is from ancestor NAICS 4451 grocery and convenience retail and may not reflect route driving, technical service, and warehouse labor in vending.
a.change_evidence: Payroll-weighted time-and-motion data from a representative set of qualifying operators, split across drivers, warehouse pickers, technicians, dispatch, sales, customer service, and administration and tagged for existing telemetry or VMS use, would materially change the range.
a.rationale: The defining workflow is already automated at the point of sale. O*NET shows that servicing still centers on filling machines, field inspection, repair, collection, transport, and installation, with a smaller information and record layer. Industry surveys also show high existing adoption of cashless payment and vending-management systems, so the not-yet-automated opportunity is bounded.

d5.bridge: NAMA's census measures the broader convenience-services ecosystem, including vending, micro markets, office coffee, and pantry service, and forecasts nominal revenue rather than constant-price quantity for the current vending-service basket. Vending Market Watch also combines vending and micro markets and reports survey-based nominal sales and locations. I discount forecast revenue growth for price inflation and format migration, using vending-machine location growth and channel diversification to support moderate real expansion. The broader-format and nominal measures overstate current-basket quantity if micro markets replace machines or prices rise, while new nonoffice locations and undercounted small operators could cause understatement.
caveats: The sources mix vending with adjacent formats and report nominal revenue.
The NAMA forecast comes from an industry body and participating companies represent about 27 percent of industry activity.
Office attendance, product inflation, client subsidies, and conversion to micro markets can separate revenue from current-basket quantity.
d5.change_evidence: A five-year panel of inflation-adjusted vending-only unit sales per machine, machines on location, account wins and losses, end-user counts, and location-type mix from payment processors and operators would materially improve the forecast.
d5.rationale: The broader channel recovered strongly, vending remained its largest business line, and reported machines on location rose 7 percent in 2024. Demand is diversifying beyond offices into manufacturing, schools, healthcare, corrections, hospitality, and residential settings. Hybrid work, format substitution, nutrition preferences, and location churn keep the downside near flat despite optimistic industry forecasts.

caveats: No source directly measures eligibility under the normalized-EBITDA and transferability lens.
Many survey respondents combine vending with micro markets, office coffee, pantry, or bottling.
The frozen LMM firm-count input is missing, so the eligible-firm count cannot be calculated.
e.change_evidence: A representative firm-level audit of NAICS 445132 businesses using contracts, route density, external-location revenue, owner dependence, normalized EBITDA, and transferability would directly determine eligibility.
e.rationale: Census defines the industry around retailing through machines the operator services, which closely matches a recurring external-location service. Most operating route businesses in the EBITDA band should qualify, with exclusions for captive routes, weak or revocable location relationships, owner-dependent micro-routes, passive assets, and businesses lacking transferable operations.

l.detail: BLS Industry Productivity (IPS) at ancestor 4451: labor compensation (L02) $116,112.7M / sectoral output (T30) $896,320.0M, year 2024

n.detail: margin 0.0540 (margins.json[445]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Retail (Grocery and Food) EBITDA/Sales 5.40%); revenue/employee $282,189 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 4451); DATA GAP: no SUSB size-class data for this code; n_band could not be computed — the research run must supply it with a shown chain, quality ESTIMATE
n.rationale: dataset n_band has no defensible value

caveats: This is a bounded judgment rather than an observed operator-retention share.
Technology can change the format from traditional vending to smart cooler or micro market without eliminating the convenience-services operator.
The estimate concerns accountable operation, not preservation of route headcount or visit frequency.
o.change_evidence: Longitudinal machine cohorts measuring host self-stocking, direct supplier fulfillment, fully autonomous replenishment, account terminations, and migrations to non-operator formats, supported by processor transaction data, would materially change this share.
o.rationale: The retail interface is already unattended, but stocked physical goods still require an accountable party to secure locations, choose assortments, procure inventory, fill and clean machines, manage food safety, settle commissions, repair equipment, and resolve failures. Smarter machines and software reduce visits and decisions but do not eliminate replenishment; some volume can migrate to host self-stocking, direct delivery, or formats outside the current operator model.

caveats: No source directly measures AI-benefit pass-through in vending contracts.
Retention varies with location exclusivity, route density, client subsidy, and whether end users or hosts bear price changes.
The estimate excludes demand changes and implementation friction.
q.change_evidence: Location-level contribution margins, commission schedules, rebid histories, subsidy terms, and same-machine prices before and after documented workflow automation would directly estimate the discounted retained share.
q.rationale: Operators control machine prices and can initially retain route, forecasting, pre-kitting, and administrative savings. Location commissions, client service demands, competitive rebids, payment fees, and low end-user switching costs pass some benefit back through price, assortment, subsidy, or higher service levels. Dense proprietary routes should retain more than commodity accounts won mainly on commission.

caveats: This is implementation judgment rather than an observed five-year realization rate.
High VMS and cashless adoption means some readily captured benefits are already in the baseline.
New AI functionality may be limited by legacy machine connectivity and data ownership.
rho.change_evidence: Matched pre/post deployments across at least 20 operators measuring paid route hours, miles, warehouse picks, stockouts, spoilage, service calls, settlement errors, and administrative hours for integrated AI forecasting and dispatch would materially revise implementation expectations.
rho.rationale: Telemetry, cashless data, and vending-management systems make forecasting, dynamic routing, pre-kitting, stock recommendations, reconciliation, and fault triage implementable within five years. Realization is constrained by mixed machine vintages, fragmented product masters, small-operator capital, weak integrations, location-specific exceptions, and the irreducible physical route.

s5.bridge: A 2022 operator survey reported that almost half of respondents were involved in acquiring or divesting routes or parts of operations, which measures broad route activity rather than qualifying firm-level external control transfers over a five-year denominator. Census separately reports 51 percent of responding U.S. employer-business owners across all industries were 55 or older in 2018. I discount route-part activity, repeated transactions, closures, internal transfers, and non-LMM participants to infer a five-year firm-control probability. Survey selection and inclusion of partial routes push the proxy upward; undercoverage of private retirements and subsequent consolidation can push it downward.
caveats: The operator survey is not a probability sample and combines vending with adjacent convenience services.
The owner-age evidence is all-industry, owner-level, and from data year 2018.
The evidence does not provide a unique eligible-firm denominator or isolate external control changes.
s5.change_evidence: Five years of closed transactions from industry brokers, DealStats, BizBuySell, Axial, SBA lenders, and operator records, distinguishing whole-company control sales from route fragments and matched to eligible firms at the start of each year, would materially change the estimate.
s5.rationale: Routes are divisible and consolidators can tuck them into denser networks, while owner aging and survey-reported buying or selling support meaningful turnover. However, a location list or equipment package is not necessarily a transferable operating company, so the base remains well below the broad route-activity share.

principal_driver: Recurring external-location relationships combine scalable route data with physical replenishment that preserves accountable operator demand.
principal_weakness: Most remaining labor is physical, while high existing adoption of cashless and vending-management technology reduces the genuinely incremental automation pool.

sources.supports: Defines the industry as establishments retailing merchandise through vending machines that they service and identifies excluded machine categories.
sources.title: 2022 NAICS Definition: 445132 Vending Machine Operators

sources.supports: Lists tasks including filling, inspection, testing, repair, maintenance records, transaction records, cash collection, settlement, service calls, transport, and installation.
sources.title: O*NET OnLine: Coin, Vending, and Amusement Machine Servicers and Repairers

sources.supports: Reports that 74 percent of respondents ran one to ten routes, almost half were acquiring or divesting routes or route parts, VMS use reached 77 percent, and remote data collection reached 47 percent in 2022.
sources.title: State of the Industry for Vending and Micro Market Report: 2022 Was a Year of Continued Recovery

sources.supports: Reports 2.3 million vending machines on location in 2024, up 7 percent; 60 percent of operators increased locations; more than 70 percent of machines accepted cashless payments; and operator size and consolidation evidence.
sources.title: Technology and Convenience Fuel Growth: 2024 State of the Vending and Micro Market Industry

sources.supports: Reports $31.1 billion of broader convenience-services revenue in 2025, 8.1 percent average annual growth since 2023, vending as the largest business line, and a 6.5 percent annual growth forecast; participants represented about 27 percent of activity.
sources.title: NAMA State of Convenience Services Report Release

sources.supports: Reports that 51 percent of responding owners of employer businesses were 55 or older, 43 percent were 35 to 54, and 6 percent were 34 or younger, based on the 2019 ABS with data year 2018.
sources.title: Business Owners' Ages

title: Vending Machine Operators
